subject: ap-government
theme: Political participation in the United States has expanded through constitutional amendments and legislation that broadened voting rights, while structural features such as the winner-take-all Electoral College and campaign finance law shape how parties, interest groups, and media compete for influence over electoral and policy outcomes.

core_concepts.title: Expansion of Voting Rights
core_concepts.detail: The franchise has expanded in waves: the 15th Amendment (1870, race), 19th Amendment (1920, women), 24th Amendment (1964, poll taxes), 26th Amendment (1971, age 18), and Voting Rights Act of 1965 — each represents Congress or the Constitution removing barriers to popular sovereignty.

core_concepts.title: Two-Party System & Duverger's Law
core_concepts.detail: The two-party system persists primarily because of plurality (winner-take-all) voting rules — under Duverger's Law, single-member districts with plurality rules produce two-party systems because voters avoid 'wasting' votes on third parties that cannot win.

core_concepts.title: Citizens United & Super PACs
core_concepts.detail: Campaign finance law pivots on Citizens United v. FEC (2010): the Supreme Court ruled corporations and unions have First Amendment rights to spend unlimited amounts on independent political expenditures, creating Super PACs and fundamentally changing money in politics.

core_concepts.title: Interest Group Lobbying Strategies
core_concepts.detail: Interest groups influence policy through direct lobbying (personal contact with officials) and grassroots mobilization (organizing citizens to pressure officials from outside) — elitist theory argues well-organized, well-funded groups hold disproportionate power, undermining pluralist competition.

core_concepts.title: Electoral College Structure & Rules
core_concepts.detail: The Electoral College allocates 538 electors among states by Congressional delegation size; most states use winner-take-all rules, which amplify pluralities into complete state electoral victories and concentrate campaign resources on swing states.

core_concepts.title: Media Agenda-Setting & Framing
core_concepts.detail: Media shapes politics through agenda-setting (determining which issues get public attention) and framing (how issues are presented) — new media and social media have fragmented the traditional media's agenda-setting power, enabling political actors to communicate directly with audiences.

core_concepts.title: Incumbency Advantage in Elections
core_concepts.detail: Incumbency advantage compounds political inequality: incumbents benefit from name recognition, the franking privilege, established fundraising networks, and often gerrymandered districts — reelection rates for House incumbents routinely exceed 90%.

key_terms.definition: Organizations of like-minded people that seek to win elections and control government in order to influence public policy
An organized group of people with shared policy goals that tries to influence government decisions, usually by lobbying, without running candidates for office
The tendency of a voter to align with and consistently vote for a particular political party based on personal identity and values
Funds donated directly to a political party for 'party-building' activities — largely unregulated before the Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act of 2002
Independent political committees that can raise and spend unlimited funds from corporations, unions, and individuals to influence elections, so long as they do not coordinate with campaigns
The process by which a state's registered voters in a party vote to select delegates to the national convention or choose among candidates for the party's presidential nomination
The body of 538 electors chosen by states to formally elect the President and Vice President; each state gets electors equal to its Congressional delegation
The process by which interest groups attempt to influence legislators and government officials by making direct contact, providing information, and building relationships
The mass media's power to focus public attention on certain issues, effectively determining which topics citizens and officials consider important
The 1920 constitutional amendment granting women the right to vote, completing decades of suffrage movement organizing
A system in which two major political parties dominate elections, maintained by plurality voting rules and ballot access laws that disadvantage third parties
The 2010 Supreme Court case that ruled corporations and unions have First Amendment rights to spend unlimited funds on independent political expenditures
The increased advantage incumbents have over challengers, including name recognition, franking privilege, fundraising networks, and gerrymandered districts

exam_tip: The AP exam heavily tests the connection between Citizens United v. FEC and the First Amendment — know that the Court extended free speech rights to corporations and unions for independent expenditures (not direct contributions), and always connect campaign finance questions to the distinction between Super PACs (unlimited independent spending) and direct candidate contributions (still regulated).
